zotero.abstractNote: Distance encoding (DE) has emerged as a powerful technique to enhance the expressive power of graph neural networks (GNNs) by injecting structural information into node representations. However, existing DE-based GNNs often suffer from high computational complexity, as they typically require pre-computing all-pairs shortest path distances or rely on expensive sampling strategies. In this paper, we propose Recurrent Distance-Encoding Neural Networks (RDEN), a novel framework that learns to encode distances in a recurrent manner. RDEN avoids the need for explicit distance pre-computation by iteratively updating distance-aware representations through message passing. We theoretically analyze the expressive power of RDEN and show that it can distinguish non-isomorphic graphs that are indistinguishable by 1-WL. Extensive experiments on various graph learning benchmarks demonstrate that RDEN achieves competitive performance while being significantly more efficient than existing DE-based methods.
zotero.proceedingsTitle: Proceedings of the 41st International Conference on Machine Learning
